revision.text:
[[ar:الإمبراطور كوتوكو]][[arz:الامبراطور كوتوكو]][[azb:کوتوکو (ایمپیراتور)]][[be:Імператар Котаку]][[ca:Kōtoku Tennō]][[ckb:ئیمپراتۆر کۆتۆکو]][[cs:Kótoku]][[de:Kōtoku]][[en:Emperor Kōtoku]][[es:Kōtoku Tennō]][[fa:امپراتور کوتوکو]][[fr:Kōtoku]][[id:Kaisar Kōtoku]][[it:Kōtoku]][[ja:孝徳天皇]][[ka:კოტოკუ]][[ko:고토쿠 천황]][[mr:कोतोकू]][[nl:Kotoku]][[pl:Kōtoku]][[pt:Kotoku]][[ru:Император Котоку]][[sh:Car Kōtoku]][[simple:Emperor Kōtoku]][[sl:Cesar Kotoku]][[sv:Kotoku]][[th:จักรพรรดิโคโตกุ]][[tl:Emperador Kōtoku]][[tr:Kōtoku (imparator)]][[uk:Імператор Котоку]][[uz:Imperator Kōtoku]][[vi:Thiên hoàng Kōtoku]][[wuu:孝德天皇]][[yo:Emperor Kōtoku]][[zh-min-nan:Kôtoku Thian-hông]][[zh-yue:孝德天皇]][[zh:孝德天皇]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Emperor Kotoku.jpg|birth_date=0596|death_date={{death date|0654|11|24}}|death_place={{ill|Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|qid=Q11659570|1=Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|monarch|qid=Q116|1=Monarch}}; {{ill|ruler|qid=Q1097498|1=ruler}}|title={{ill|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|1=Emperor of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Hashihito|qid=Q11655373|1=Princess Hashihito}}; {{ill|Q106164352|qid=Q106164352|1=Soga no Chichina}}; {{ill|Q11463822|qid=Q11463822|1=Koashihime}}|children={{ill|Arima|qid=Q11516721|1=Arima}}|father={{ill|Chinu|qid=Q11617199|1=Chinu}}|mother={{ill|Princess Kibitsu-Hime|qid=Q11412643|1=Princess Kibihime}}|relatives={{ill|Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}|family={{ill|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}|native_name=孝徳天皇|resting_place={{ill|Yamada Uenoyama Kofun|qid=Q48752311|1=Yamada Uenoyama Kofun}}}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2012}} {{Infobox Monarch | name = Emperor Kōtoku
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|孝徳天皇}}}} | title = [[Okimi|Great King of Yamato]] | succession = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} | image = | caption = | reign = 645–654 (AD) | coronation = | predecessor = [[:en:Kōgyoku|Kōgyoku]] | successor = [[:en:Saimei|Saimei]] | posthumous name = [[Posthumous name#Chinese-style (Han-style) shigō|Chinese-style ''shigō'']]:
Emperor Kōtoku ({{lang|ja|孝徳天皇}})

[[Posthumous name#Japanese-style shigō|Japanese-style ''shigō'']]:
Ameyorozutoyohi no Sumeramikoto ({{lang|ja|天万豊日天皇}}) | spouse = {{ill|Princess Hashihito|en|Princess Hashihito|qid=Q11655373|lt=Hashihito|1=Princess Hashihito}} | issue = Prince Arima | royal house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} | father = Prince Chinu | mother = Princess Kibitsu-hime | birth_name = Karu ({{lang|ja|軽}}) | birth_date = 596 (AD) | birth_place = | death_date = {{death year and age|654|596}} | death_place = Toyosaki no Miya (Ōsaka) | place of burial = ''Ōsaka-no-shinaga no misasagi'' (大阪磯長陵) (Osaka)| }} {{nihongo|'''Emperor Kōtoku'''|孝徳天皇|Kōtoku-[[:en:Tennō|Tennō]]|596 – November 24, 654 AD}} was the 36th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}},{{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/036/index.html 孝徳天皇 (33)] according to the traditional {{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=order of succession|1=List of emperors of Japan}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 50. The years of his reign lasted from {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=645 through 654|1=Asuka period}}.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 266–267; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki''. p. 132–133; Titsinh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon'', pp. 47–30.|page=47}} ==Traditional narrative== Before Kōtoku's ascension to the {{ill|Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q1981690|lt=Chrysanthemum Throne|1=Chrysanthemum Throne}}, his personal name (''[[:en:Imina|Imina]]'')Brown, pp. 264; prior to {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}}, the personal names of the emperors (their ''imina'') were very long and people did not generally use them; however, the number of characters in each name diminished after Jomei's reign. was {{nihongo|Karu|軽}} or {{nihongo|Prince Karu|軽皇子|Karu-no-Ōji}}.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 8. He was a descendant of [[Emperor Bidatsu]]. He was a son of Chinu no ōkimi (Prince Chinu, 茅渟王) by Kibitsuhime no ōkimi (Princess Kibitsuhime, 吉備姫王). {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}} was his elder sister from the same parents. Chinu was a son of Prince Oshisaka hikohito no ōe, whose father was the Emperor Bidatsu. He had at least three consorts including his Empress, Hashihito no Himemiko (Princess Hashihito), the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}} and his sister Empress Kōgyoku. In the 3rd year of Kōgyoku''-tennō''{{'}}s reign (皇極天皇三年), the empress abdicated; and the succession (''senso'') was received by her younger brother. Shortly thereafter, Emperor Kōtoku is said to have acceded to the throne (''sokui'').Titsingh, pp. 47–48; Brown, p. 266; Varley, p. 44; a distinct act of ''senso'' is unrecognized prior to {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}; and all sovereigns except {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|qid=Q349280|lt=Yōzei|1=Emperor Yōzei}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, and {{ill|Emperor Fushimi|en|Emperor Fushimi|qid=Q373949|lt=Fushimi|1=Emperor Fushimi}} have ''senso'' and ''sokui'' in the same year until the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|qid=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami|1=Emperor Go-Murakami}}. Kōtoku ruled from July 12, 645 (AD),July 12 645 corresponds to the Fourteenth Day of the Sixth Month of 645 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|qid=Q246658|lt=isshi|1=Sexagenary cycle}}). until his death in 654. In 645, he ascended to the throne two days after Prince {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Naka no Ōe|1=Emperor Tenji}} (Emperor Tenji) assassinated {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|qid=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}} in the court of Kōgyoku. Kōgyoku abdicated in favor of her son and crown prince, Naka no Ōe, but Naka no Ōe insisted Kōtoku should ascend to the throne instead. Kōtoku's contemporary title would not have been ''tennō'', as most historians {{whom?|date=November 2018}} believe this title was not introduced until the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}. Rather, it was presumably ''Sumeramikoto'' or ''Amenoshita Shiroshimesu Ōkimi'' (治天下大王), meaning "the great king who rules all under heaven". Alternatively, Kōtoku might have been referred to as (ヤマト大王/大君) or the "Great King of Yamato". According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'', he was of gentle personality and was in favor of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. In 645, he created a new city in the area called {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Naniwa|1=Osaka}}, and moved the capital from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} to this new city (see {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}). The new capital had a seaport and was good for foreign trade and diplomatic activities. In 653, Kōtoku sent an envoy to the court of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, but some of the ships were lost ''en route''. Naka no Ōe held the Rank of crown prince and was the ''de facto'' leader of the government. In 653 Naka no Ōe proposed to move the capital again to Yamato province. Kōtoku denied. Naka no Ōe ignored the emperor's policy and moved to the former province. Many courtiers of the court, including Empress Hashihito, followed him. Kōtoku was left in the palace. In the next year he died of an illness. After his death, Naka would not ascend to the throne. Instead, his mother and the sister of Kōtoku, the former Empress Kogyoku, ascended to the throne under another name, Empress Saimei. He enacted the [[Taika Reform]] edicts. The system of ''hasshō kyakkan'' (eight ministries and a hundred offices) was first established during the reign of Emperor Kōtoku.Varley, p. 133. [[File:Osakano shinagano misasagi.jpg|thumb|right|Memorial Shinto shrine and mausoleum honoring Emperor Kōtoku]] The actual site of Kōtoku's {{ill|Grave|en|Grave|qid=Q173387|lt=grave|1=Grave}} is known. This emperor is traditionally venerated at a {{ill|Memorial|en|Memorial|qid=Q5003624|lt=Memorial|1=Memorial}} [[Shinto]] [[shrine]] (''misasagi'') at Osaka. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates this location as Kōtoku's {{ill|Mausoleum|en|Mausoleum|qid=Q162875|lt=Mausoleum|1=Mausoleum}}. It is formally named ''Ōsaka-no-shinaga no misasagi''.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 420. Traditionally the monk {{ill|Hōdō|en|Hōdō|qid=Q11555373|lt=Hōdō|1=Hōdō}}, was born in today India, and traveled to Japan via Tang China and the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] kingdom in Korea. Hōdō cured an illness of the [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (596 – 654 AD), who then sent the monk to establish numerous Buddhist temples. According to legend Hōdō founded {{ill|Tenjō-ji|en|Tenjō-ji|qid=Q3518099|lt=Tenjō-ji|1=Tenjō-ji}} in 646 (AD).{{cite encyclopedia |year=2012 |title=法道 |encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |language=Japanese |trans-title=Hōdō |accessdate=2012-10-03 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25}} The monk is worshipped in the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]]. ===''Kugyō''=== ''Kugyō'' (Kugyō) is a collective term for the very few most powerful men attached to the court of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} in pre-{{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} eras. In general, this elite group included only three to four men at a time. These were hereditary courtiers whose experience and background would have brought them to the pinnacle of a life's career. During Kōtoko's reign, this apex of the ''[[Daijō-kan]]'' included: * ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', Abe no Kurahashi-maro (阿部倉梯麻呂) (d. 649), 645–649.Brown, p. 266. * ''Sadaijin'', Kose no Tokoda (巨勢徳太) (593–658), 649–658. * ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', Soga no Kura-no-Yamada no Ishikawa-no-maro (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂) (d. 649), 645–649. * ''Udaijin'', Ōtomo no Nagatoko (大伴長徳) (d. 651), 649–651. * ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}''(内臣), Nakatomi Kamako (中臣鎌子) ({{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|qid=Q433995|lt=Fujiwara no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}, 藤原鎌足) (614–669), 645–669. ==Eras of Kōtoku's reign== The years of Kōtoku's reign are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''.Titsingh, p. 47. * ''{{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|qid=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}}'' (645–650) * ''{{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|qid=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi|1=Hakuchi (era)}}'' (650–655) ==Consorts and children== Empress: {{ill|Princess Hashihito|en|Princess Hashihito|qid=Q11655373|lt=Princess Hashihito|1=Princess Hashihito}} (間人皇女, d. 665), {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}} and [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]]'s daughter ''Hi'': Abe no Otarashi-hime (阿部小足媛), Abe no Kurahashi-maro's daughter * Prince Arima (有間皇子, 640–658) ''Hi'': Saga no Chi-no-iratsume (蘇我乳娘), Soga no Kura-no-Yamada no Ishikawa-no-maro's daughter ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |boxstyle_5=background-color: #9fe; |ref={{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e5%a4%a9%e7%9a%87%e5%ae%b6#emp036|title=Genealogy|website=Reichsarchiv|date=April 30, 2010 |access-date=25 January 2018|language=ja}} |1= 1. '''Emperor Kōtoku''' |2= 2. [[:ja:茅渟王|Prince Chinu]] |3= 3. [[:ja:吉備姫王|Princess Kibitsu-hime]] (d. 643) |4= 4. Prince [[:ja:押坂彦人大兄皇子|Oshisakanohikohito-no-Ōe]] |5= 5. Princess [[:ja:糠手姫皇女|Nukate-hime]] (c. 570–664) |6= 6. [[:ja:桜井皇子|Sakurai no Miko]] (560–587) |7= |8= 8.[[Emperor Bidatsu]] (538–585) |9= 9. [[:ja:広姫|Hiro-hime]] (d. c. 575) |10=10. [[Emperor Bidatsu]] (538–585) |11=11. Unako no Otoshi |12=12. {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} (509–571) |13=13. [[Soga no Kitashihime]] |14= |15= }} ==See also== * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * {{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=List of emperors of Japan|1=List of emperors of Japan}} * {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|qid=Q3271557|lt=Imperial cult|1=Imperial cult}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George.]] (1896). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''.] London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner. {{OCLC|448337491}} * Brown, Delmer M. and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac.|1=Isaac Titsingh}} (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{s-start}} {{s-reg}} {{s-bef|before={{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=List of emperors of Japan}}:
Kōtoku|years=645–654}} {{s-aft|after={{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}}} {{s-end}} {{Emperors of Japan}} < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < < {{Kofun navbar}} [[en:Emperor Kōtoku]] [[ar:الإمبراطور كوتوكو]] [[arz:الامبراطور كوتوكو]] [[azb:کوتوکو (ایمپیراتور)]] [[be:Імператар Котаку]] [[ca:Kōtoku Tennō]] [[ckb:ئیمپراتۆر کۆتۆکو]] [[cs:Kótoku]] [[de:Kōtoku]] [[es:Kōtoku Tennō]] [[fa:امپراتور کوتوکو]] [[fr:Kōtoku]] [[id:Kaisar Kōtoku]] [[it:Kōtoku]] [[ja:孝徳天皇]] [[ka:კოტოკუ]] [[ko:고토쿠 천황]] [[mr:कोतोकू]] [[nl:Kotoku]] [[pl:Kōtoku]] [[pt:Kotoku]] [[ru:Император Котоку]] [[sh:Car Kōtoku]] [[simple:Emperor Kōtoku]] [[sl:Cesar Kotoku]] [[sv:Kotoku]] [[th:จักรพรรดิโคโตกุ]] [[tl:Emperador Kōtoku]] [[tr:Kōtoku (imparator)]] [[uk:Імператор Котоку]] [[uz:Imperator Kōtoku]] [[vi:Thiên hoàng Kōtoku]] [[wuu:孝德天皇]] [[yo:Emperor Kōtoku]] [[zh:孝德天皇]] [[zh-min-nan:Kôtoku Thian-hông]] [[zh-yue:孝德天皇]] {{wikidata link|Q317980}} [[Category:596 births]] [[Category:654 deaths]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:7th-century Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Emperors of Japan]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
